note_html:
Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 54-year-old male, presents with a complaint of stabbing, shooting pain in the right cheekbone area of his face, which has been ongoing for approximately 2-3 months. He reports that the pain is exacerbated by certain temperatures and can be triggered by cold weather. He denies numbness or tingling in the affected area and reports that his smile and vision are unchanged. He describes the pain as excruciating and rates it as a 10 out of 10 in severity. He has tried ibuprofen, Motrin, and topical treatments such as tiger balm, which have provided minimal relief.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of occasional headaches, for which he takes Tylenol, but denies a history of migraines or brain tumors.

Review of systems: The patient reports no symptoms in other organs or systems.

Current medications: He takes Tylenol for occasional headaches.

Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.

Physical exam: The patient was able to follow my finger in both directions, and his smile appeared symmetric. He reported a little numbness on the right side of his face when I rubbed my fingers next to his ear. When I used a cotton tape to run along the side of his face, he reported feeling a little numb on the right side. When I poked him with a needle on the right side of his face, he reported feeling dull, whereas on the left side, he reported feeling sharp.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has trigeminal neuralgia, a condition where compression of the trigeminal nerve by a small blood vessel causes shooting, electrical pain in the face.

Problem list: Trigeminal neuralgia; confirmed.

Plan:
I prescribed Tegretol 100mg orally 3 times a day for the treatment of trigeminal neuralgia. I explained the potential side effects of the medication, including memory loss, tingling, imbalance, and dermatologic reactions, particularly in individuals with Eustachian tube dysfunction. I recommended genetic testing to ensure the medication is safe for the patient. I instructed the patient to follow up in one month to assess the efficacy of the medication and to discuss further treatment options if necessary.

Follow-up: The patient will return in one month to assess the efficacy of the Tegretol and to discuss further treatment options if necessary.
